Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 28 and juggling a lot, so I'd usually carve out time when I could—late nights mostly, after everything else settled down. I'd try to dedicate chunks of time on weekends, but it never felt consistent. I relied heavily on reading materials and writing notes because that's how things stick for me. Sometimes I'd binge-study for hours, other times I'd barely look at anything for days.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule even when I tried—work stuff, family obligations, they all got in the way. When assignments were due, I'd suddenly find myself studying way more intensely. The weeks without deadlines? I'd drift and barely touch anything, even though I knew I should.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. I wasn't one to browse around for fun—it was more functional, like checking what needed to be done. The anxiety of potentially missing something important kept me coming back.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like something I had to force myself to do. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more regularly, but then life would get busy and I'd fall off. The closest it got to routine was checking it every few days to make sure I wasn't missing deadlines.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing things out. I'd download PDFs and articles and actually engage with those pretty deeply. The videos were helpful too—I watched a lot of them—but I preferred when there were transcripts or written summaries I could annotate. Anything I could highlight and take notes on felt worthwhile.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. Never posted once, even though I saw other people engaging. It felt performative to me, like I was supposed to say something just to prove I was participating. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary materials that weren't directly tied to assignments—I just didn't have the bandwidth to explore extra stuff when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I needed to submit or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. I needed to see the practical payoff. If something felt like busywork or padding, I'd mentally check out even if I technically completed it. The mindfulness exercises I did actually helped with my stress, so those felt valuable in a different way.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what counted toward my grade. Required stuff got done no matter what—that's why my assignment completion is high—but optional things fell to the bottom of the list. It's not that I didn't see their value; I just couldn't justify the time investment when I was already stretched thin.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download readings and study from printed copies or saved files all the time. I'd make handwritten notes from the materials and review those instead of going back to the LMS. Sometimes I'd use external resources like YouTube videos or articles I found through Google to supplement what we were learning. A lot of my actual studying happened completely offline.
Q10: Most of my real learning happened away from the computer, honestly. I'd print out materials, sit somewhere quiet, and read through them multiple times with a pen in hand. Or I'd rewrite my notes to help memorize concepts. The LMS would just show that I downloaded something once, but it wouldn't capture the hours I spent with that material afterward. My engagement looked lower than it actually was.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video or reading once, download it or take screenshots of key parts, and then work with it offline for days. My internet access isn't always reliable, and honestly, I focus better when I'm not on the computer with all its distractions. One download might represent a week's worth of study, but the system only sees that single access.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Around midterms, I felt overwhelmed by the workload across all my courses—I'm taking 18 online courses, which is insane. My attendance dropped because I was trying to triage what was most urgent. Sometimes I'd open the LMS, see everything I needed to do, and just close it because it was too much.
Q13: Constantly. With low resources, I'm always balancing work and school, and sometimes work has to win because I need the money. Fatigue was a huge factor—by the time I'd get to studying, my brain was already fried. The LMS became something I'd engage with in survival mode rather than with genuine curiosity. Confusion about assignments also made me hesitant to start things because I didn't want to waste time doing them wrong.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching videos or scrolling through materials but not really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere—worrying about bills, thinking about other deadlines, just generally anxious. I'd technically be logged in and active, but I wasn't really present. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts indefinitely because they stressed me out—I never felt like I had anything smart enough to say. I also avoided starting assignments until the last minute even when I knew the material, because the anxiety of doing it wrong paralyzed me. The mindfulness stuff I learned helped a bit with this, but procrastination was definitely my coping mechanism for feeling overwhelmed.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd have low activity for weeks and then suddenly be all over the LMS in the days before something was due. My response time to assignments averaged over 11 hours because I'd often submit late at night after procrastinating all day. Without deadlines, I'd drift—they were the only thing that created urgency for me to actually engage.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me anxious about the next assignment. I'd read feedback carefully and try to incorporate it, but I wouldn't necessarily go back to the LMS more often because of it. It influenced how I approached work more than how frequently I logged in.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because without rigid structure, I'd drift. The modules being available all at once meant I could binge when needed, but it also meant I could ignore things for too long. I needed more external accountability than the structure provided.
Q19: Having everything online made it accessible, which I needed given my situation. But the lack of in-person interaction made it easy to feel disconnected and drift. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic, which is partly why I never engaged with them. Clearer expectations and maybe more structured check-ins would have helped me stay on track better.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—clicking around, watching things, downloading stuff, but not actually producing work because of anxiety or confusion about what's expected. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just spinning your wheels, trying to figure things out or avoiding the actual hard work of completing assignments. The LMS sees clicks, not comprehension.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience in some ways. I completed almost all my assignments and passed despite my attendance being under 50%. A lot of my learning happened offline with downloaded materials and personal notes. The LMS activity is just a fraction of actual engagement—someone could be deeply engaged with the content without it showing up in the logs at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you're clicking on, how often you submit things. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually thinking deeply about something or just skimming. They miss offline study, emotional state, life circumstances, and whether someone is genuinely learning versus just completing tasks to survive.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when the material connects to something meaningful for you and you find yourself thinking about it even when you're not studying. For me, engagement is internal—it's about curiosity and investment, not just activity metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was genuinely interested in some of the course content even though my attendance was poor. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials or handwritten notes. It definitely wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me feel better about the whole experience. My engagement was real, just not always visible in the system.
